title: Chapter 11: The Sancerre
content: The restaurant has no name. I'm not being mysterious — it literally has no sign, no awning, no indication that anything exists behind the weathered green door off rue de Rivoli except maybe a storage closet for the lingerie shop next door. But behind the door there are ten tables, napkins folded into swans, and a wine list they'll fine you ten thousand euros for photographing. I know this because Timeline B Avery once watched someone get thrown out for trying.

Timeline B Avery, who was supposed to be here right now, meeting a fashion contact whose name I never learned because she canceled via a one-line text forty minutes ago: *Rainy day for champagne, non?* And now I'm sitting alone at a corner table with a glass of overpriced Pouilly-Fumé and the particular kind of loneliness that hits different in Paris — where everyone around you is kissing and laughing and sharing desserts and you're just a girl with a canceled appointment and a reality-hopping app hidden in your coat.

The waiter brings bread. I tear off a piece. The crust cracks between my fingers — warm, brittle, dusted with flour that coats my thumb and forefinger. I eat it without tasting it. The rain has stopped but the window is still beaded, each droplet catching candlelight like a small trapped sun.
The door opens.

I don't look up. I'm reading the wine list for the third time, memorizing vintages I'll never order, because that's the kind of person I am now — someone who memorizes wine lists in empty restaurants instead of having actual conversations. The footsteps cross the room. A chair scrapes. Then another chair scrapes, ten feet away, at the table beside mine.
I look up.

Julian B is settling into his seat like he belongs there, like he reserved it, which — he must have. The restaurant only takes reservations. He unfolds his napkin across his lap with a motion so practised it's almost absent, and then he looks at me. No surprise. No pretense of coincidence. He lifts the glass of water the waiter has just poured and raises it toward me across the gap between our tables.
The audacity.
"You're not serious," I say.
"Completely serious." He takes a sip. "The duck here is extraordinary, if you're wondering."
"I wasn't wondering. I was wondering how you knew I'd be here."
"Your contact canceled at six fifteen. I know because she told my contact she wasn't coming, and my contact told me, and I thought — " He sets the glass down. " — that's convenient."
"You booked the table next to mine."
"I booked both tables adjacent to yours. The other one's empty." He gestures to the table on his other side. "I wanted to make sure we weren't interrupted."
"By what?"
"By anyone who might think we're not already together."

My throat tightens. The candle between us flickers, and the shadows shift across his face — the angle of his jaw, the dark eyes watching me with that particular Julian B intensity that's different from Julian A's edge and Julian C's warmth. This one's patient. Like he's got all the time in the world and every intention of using it.
The waiter appears. He looks at me, then at Julian, then back at me, and his expression settles into the knowing smile of a man who's seen this exact scene ten thousand times. "Ensemble?"
I should say no. I should gesture to my separate table, my separate wine, my separate life that has nothing to do with the man ten feet away.
"Ensemble," Julian says.

He says it easily, fluidly, the French rounding his consonants the way it does when you grew up between languages — not learning them, but inhaling them. The waiter nods and moves our candles together, pulls the tables flush, and suddenly we're not two people in the same room. We're two people at the same table.
I don't correct him.

Julian orders before I can process what's happening. Sancerre — not the Pouilly-Fumé I've been nursing, something else, something with a vintage that makes the waiter nod approvingly. Oysters. A truffle dish I don't catch the name of because I'm still stuck on the fact that I didn't say no.
"You ordered for me," I say.
"I ordered for the table. You can refuse anything you don't want."
"I don't like truffle."
"You do." He tilts his head. "You just don't know it yet."

The Sancerre arrives first. He pours it, and the smell hits me before the glass reaches my hand — grapefruit and limestone and something green, like rain on cold stone. It's crystalline, almost aggressively clean, the kind of wine that doesn't bother being warm. I sip. The acidity blooms across my tongue, bright and mineral, and underneath it a faint citrus finish that lingers.
"Good?" he asks, in French, because of course he speaks it like he was born to it, because he was — born to it and two other languages and a last name that opens every door in this city.
"Good," I admit.

The oysters come on a bed of seaweed and shaved ice. They're small and perfect, their shells glistening. Julian squeezes lemon over the first one and slides the plate toward me, and I take it — the brine hitting my tongue like a wave, cold and sharp and alive, the taste of the sea concentrated into something that barely needs chewing.

I'm watching his hands. I can't help it. Long fingers, no rings. The way he holds the oyster fork — precise, unhurried. And there, on his left knuckle — a thin scar, maybe two centimeters, raised and silvery in the candlelight. Julian A doesn't have that scar. Julian C doesn't have that scar. Different wounds for different lives. I wonder what cut him. I wonder if the Julian who wasn't cut is luckier or just cut differently somewhere I haven't noticed yet.
"You're staring," he says.
"Your scar." I point with my chin. "How'd you get it?"

He glances at his own hand like he forgot it was there. "Piano wire. I was twelve and trying to restring a grand piano. My mother was horrified — not that I was bleeding, but that I touched the wire without gloves."
"That's the most Deveraux sentence I've ever heard."
His mouth twitches. Not quite a smile. "You haven't heard enough Deveraux sentences yet."

The truffle dish comes — shaved black over something creamy, something I don't identify because the smell overtakes everything else, earthy and dark and obscene in a way that makes me understand why people bankrupt themselves over it. I take a bite. He's right. I do like truffle.

We eat for a while without talking. It's not uncomfortable. The restaurant fills around us — a couple by the window, a solo man with a newspaper, two women sharing a dessert and a secret. The noise settles into a hum of French and clinking silverware, the candle wax melting slowly down the bottle it's stuck to.

"So," Julian says, and something in his tone shifts. The playfulness recedes. What replaces it is quieter, more deliberate, like he's choosing his words from a vault he doesn't usually open. "What did you want to be before you wanted this?"

The question lands wrong-footed. I open my mouth and nothing comes out, because I don't know what Timeline B Avery wanted before fashion. I haven't checked. I've been so careful with the big details — the career trajectory, the apartment, the social circle — that I never thought to memorize the girl who existed before the girl I'm pretending to be.
"I wanted to write," I say.

It comes out before I can stop it. Not a lie — I did want to write, once, in a timeline that feels increasingly far away. But not this timeline's truth. This is me improvising, borrowing from a life that isn't this one, and the terrifying thing is how natural it feels. How close to real.
Julian sets down his fork. Something shifts in his expression — not suspicion but surprise. Genuine surprise, the kind that rearranges the lines of his face into something younger, less guarded.
"You don't seem like someone who gave that up," he says.

The second time. The second time he's seen through my cover like it's glass. First at the gallery, now here. I'm getting sloppy, or he's getting sharper, or both — the gap between who I'm pretending to be and who I am narrowing in ways I can't control.
"Fashion pays better," I say, and the joke lands flat, and we both know it.

He lets the silence sit for a moment. Then he picks up his wine, takes a long sip, and when he speaks again, his voice has dropped — not to a whisper, but to something private, the kind of volume you use when you're about to hand someone a weapon and trust they won't use it.

"The Deveraux family," he says. "Old money. Old expectations. Old cages." He turns the glass by its stem. "My mother has had my wedding planned since I was fourteen. The venue, the flowers, the guest list. She updates it every year like it's a living document."
"Who's the bride?"
"You know who."
Celeste. I don't say it. I don't have to.

"Celeste Montague," he confirms. "Merge the empires. Combine the portfolios. Produce heirs with the right cheekbones and the right trust funds." He says it without anger, which is worse. Just the flat recitation of a future that was decided before he had a voice in it. "My father calls it 'consolidation.' My mother calls it 'destiny.' I call it a cage with better wallpaper."
"What do you want?"
The question leaves my mouth before I've approved it. He looks at me — really looks, the way he looked at the scar on his knuckle, like he's cataloguing something he doesn't want to forget.

"I want to open a gallery," he says. "Not another Deveraux monument. Something small. A space for artists nobody's heard of yet. The ones painting in basements and selling on street corners because no gallerist will look at them." His hand moves as he speaks — a gesture that's almost magnetic, pulling shapes from the air. "No curation by last name. No opening nights for collectors. Just — a door. A way in."

The candlelight catches his eyes and I see it: the gallery, clear as if he's painted it on the wall between us. White walls. Concrete floors. A kid with paint on their hands standing in front of their first canvas, and Julian standing beside them, saying *this one, this is the one*.
"They want me to be a museum," he says. "I want to be a door."

I'm staring at him again. I can't help it. Because this — this is the most honest he's been with anyone. I can feel it in the gravity of the confession, in the way he's not quite meeting my eyes now, like handing me this truth cost him something. The irony sits on my chest like a stone: the one person he's confiding in is the one person who's lying about everything. Every word between us has been built on a foundation I fabricated.

The Sancerre is half-finished. I drink the rest of mine and he refills it without asking, and I let him, because I don't know how to do anything except let him keep being kind to a person who doesn't exist.
"Julian," I start, and then I stop, because what comes next? *I'm not who you think I am? I'm from a different timeline where you're a different person and I jumped realities to steal this life?*
He waits. When I don't continue, he doesn't push. He just picks up the conversation like I handed him a thread and he's choosing not to pull it.
I've never been more grateful or more ashamed.
The mood holds — fragile, specific, held between us like the bubble of warmth above the candle. And then it doesn't.

I notice the shift in his body first. The way his shoulders draw back a millimeter. The way his jaw sets, almost imperceptibly, the muscles along it tightening. He's looking at the menu, but he's not reading it.
I follow his line of sight to the bar.

Celeste Montague is perched on a stool like it's a throne, one leg crossed over the other, a glass of something amber turning slowly between her fingers. She's watching us the way a cat watches a bird it hasn't decided to kill yet — head slightly tilted, expression carved from marble, the kind of stillness that means calculation, not calm.

Julian doesn't look at her. His hand moves under the table, onto his knee, where I can see it in the gap between the tablecloth and his lap. His fingers are clenched. The knuckles whiten. The hand that was gesturing about galleries and doors and artists in basements is now a fist, and he's holding it like he's afraid of what it might do if he lets go.

The calm is a performance. I see that now. Every relaxed syllable, every easy pour of wine, every moment of vulnerability — it was real, but it was also chosen. Selected from a repertoire of selves. The gallery-dreamer Julian. The almost-honest Julian. The Julian who could be whoever he needed to be, except the one thing his family wants, because that one would kill him.
"She doesn't scare me," he says quietly. The words are for me. The stillness is for her. "She scares what I might become if I let her."

I look at Celeste. She raises her glass to no one in particular and turns back to the bar, but I know she's still watching. I know this isn't over. In a timeline or a year or a week, she'll use what she saw tonight — the two of us, heads close, sharing wine and secrets — and she'll use it precisely, because that's what she does.
Julian finishes his wine and sets the glass down with a quiet click. "I should walk you home."
I don't argue.

The street is narrow and wet and cold. My heels click on cobblestones that glisten under the streetlamps like the backs of sleeping animals, each one slick with rain that fell an hour ago and hasn't quite dried. The cold hits my bare arms and I didn't bring a coat because Timeline B Avery doesn't get cold, except she clearly does, because my skin is pebbling and my breath makes small ghosts in front of my face.

Julian walks close enough that I can smell him — not cologne, or not just cologne, but underneath it something warm and human, cedar and clean linen and the faint mineral finish of the Sancerre still on his breath. The restaurant is behind us. Celeste is behind us. The whole performative evening is behind us, and all that's left is a street and the sound of our footsteps and the way the wet cobblestones reflect the light like scattered coins.

He doesn't touch me. His hands are in his pockets. He walks on the building side, me on the street side, and I don't know if that's chivalry or habit or just the instinct of a man who grew up being told to protect things, even things that don't belong to him.
We stop at my door. The building is old, the paint flaking around the brass handle, and the streetlight above us flickers twice like it's deciding whether to stay on.

Julian turns to face me. Then he does something I don't expect — he reaches for my hand. Not to hold it. Not the way Julian A would, with intention and demand. He takes it gently, turns it over, holds it open between us like he's reading a map.
His thumb traces a line across my palm.

My entire body goes still. The cold air on my warm palm where his fingers aren't. The press of his thumb — slow, deliberate, following a line I can't see. I don't believe in palmistry. I don't believe in fate. I believe in the Fork app and glitching and the cold mathematics of probability. But right now, with his thumb drawing a path across my skin, I believe in whatever this is.
"There's something you're not telling me," he says. His voice is quiet. Not accusing. Just factual, like he's naming the weather. "I don't know what it is. But I know it's big."

I can't speak. My mouth opens and closes and no sound comes out, because he's right, and because I want to tell him more than I've ever wanted anything. That I'm not who he thinks. That the woman he shared his gallery dream with is wearing someone else's life like a borrowed dress. That every word of tenderness between us was built on a lie I chose to tell.
His thumb traces another line. My palm is tingling, my fingers curled loosely around nothing, and the whole world has narrowed to the place where his skin meets mine.
"When you're ready," he says, "I'll still be here."
He releases my hand. Steps back. The distance between us opens like a wound, and the cold air rushes in to fill the space where his warmth was.

He turns. Walks away down the narrow street, his silhouette shrinking between the buildings until the dark takes him, and I'm left standing at a door I can barely see, my hand still open, my palm still burning where he touched it.
The Fork app buzzes against my ribs.

⚠️ INTEGRITY: 76%
⚠️ TEMPORAL ECHOES: ACTIVE

I look down at my palm. The line his thumb traced is invisible, but I feel it — a phantom warmth, a path he drew across my skin that I'll carry for hours. And underneath that warmth, underneath the ache of wanting something I can't have, something else.
Accordion music. Faint. Distant.

It's not coming from the street. It's not coming from an open window or a passing car or a busker on the corner. It's coming from somewhere that isn't Paris — somewhere that isn't this timeline at all. The notes are minor-key and looping, the kind of melody you hear in dreams, and they're pressing against the inside of my skull like a memory I haven't made yet.
I close my fist. The music doesn't stop.

He said *I'll still be here*. And I believe him. That's the worst part — I believe him completely, and I'm going to destroy that belief, because the integrity is dropping and the echoes are strengthening and I don't know how much longer I can be someone he recognizes before I stop being anyone at all.
The accordion plays on. My palm burns. And I stand at a door I can't open, in a life I can't keep, and I don't go inside.
